sections.java: // java.nio for reading a whole file Path path = Path.of("data", "input.txt"); String text = Files.readString(path); List<String> lines = Files.readAllLines(path);
sections.python: # pathlib — the modern, OO way from pathlib import Path path = Path("data") / "input.txt" # / joins! text = path.read_text() lines = path.read_text().splitlines()

sections.title: The / Operator Joins Paths
sections.content: pathlib.Path overloads / so Path("data") / "sub" / "file.txt" builds an OS-correct path — no File.separator, no manual concatenation. It's the direct analog of java.nio.file.Path but more ergonomic. path.parent, path.name, path.suffix, path.exists() all just work.

sections.java: // Buffered streaming, try-with-resources try (BufferedReader r = Files.newBufferedReader(path)) { String line; while ((line = r.readLine()) != null) { process(line); } }
sections.python: # Files are iterable line-by-line, lazily with open(path, encoding="utf-8") as f: for line in f: # streams, low memory process(line.rstrip()) # with == try-with-resources (Module 07)

sections.title: Always Specify encoding=
sections.content: open(path) uses the platform default encoding — which differs between your Mac/Linux CI (UTF-8) and a Windows box (often cp1252). That's a classic "works on my machine" bug. Always pass encoding="utf-8" explicitly. Java's readString defaults to UTF-8; Python does not until you ask.

sections.java: // Writing text + create dirs Files.createDirectories(path.getParent()); Files.writeString(path, content, StandardOpenOption.CREATE);
sections.python: # mkdir parents, then write path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) path.write_text(content, encoding="utf-8") # Or stream writes with a context manager with path.open("w", encoding="utf-8") as f: f.write(content)

sections.title: Structured Data: json Is Built In
sections.content: No Jackson/Gson dependency. import json ships with Python: json.loads(text) parses to dicts/lists, json.dumps(obj, indent=2) serializes. For files, json.load(f) / json.dump(obj, f). CSV is similar via the stdlib csv module. Both are part of the "batteries included" library.

sections.question: Why is `open(path, encoding="utf-8")` preferred over `open(path)`?
options: Bare open() uses the platform default encoding, causing cross-platform bugs
sections.explanation: Without an explicit encoding, Python falls back to the OS default — UTF-8 on most Linux/Mac, but often cp1252 on Windows. That produces mojibake or UnicodeDecodeError that only appears on certain machines. Always specify encoding="utf-8", unlike Java's Files.readString which already defaults to UTF-8.
